skill.name: face-swap
skill.description: Route face-swap and character-swap requests to the right RunComfy model for still images, videos, motion transfer, or batch identity edits.
skill.summary: Choose and run RunComfy face-swap workflows for images and videos.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static shell backtick and weak-crypto detections are mostly false positives from Markdown code spans, model names, and documentation text. Confirmed risks remain because the skill enables dual-use face-swap generation, invokes the RunComfy CLI, sends media references to external model APIs, and documents local token storage. Publish only with clear synthetic-media, consent, network, and credential-handling warnings.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use face-swap workflow without technical enforcement
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly routes face replacement and deepfake-style requests. It includes consent guidance, but it also states the skill itself does not gate inputs, so misuse depends on operator judgment rather than technical controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly describe face-swap as dual-use and acknowledge that the workflow will run supplied inputs. The safety mitigation is policy guidance, not an enforced control.

medium_findings.title: External CLI processing of biometric media references
medium_findings.description: The workflow invokes the RunComfy CLI with image, audio, and video URLs. These assets may contain biometric data and are processed by external model APIs, creating privacy and consent risk even when used legitimately.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and implementation description show media URLs being sent through runcomfy to remote model APIs. The risk is inherent to the documented workflow rather than hidden malware behavior.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted remote media can influence generation results
medium_findings.description: The skill recognizes reference image, audio, and video URLs as untrusted inputs and warns about indirect prompt injection through assets. This can cause output divergence or unexpected identity and motion behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk is explicitly documented in the skill. The exact exploitability depends on the external model pipeline and the supplied media.

low_findings.title: Markdown backtick detections are false positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick executions, but the reviewed instances are Markdown code spans, model identifiers, and shell examples. No evidence found of Ruby execution or hidden command substitution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears in Markdown documentation and fenced command examples. I did not find evidence of executable Ruby code in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Package-manager examples exceed the declared RunComfy allow-list
low_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access for runcomfy commands, but setup documentation includes npm and npx examples. These appear intended for installation, not routine operation, yet agents should not execute them without explicit user approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and legitimate installation guidance, but they sit outside the declared Bash allow-list. The risk is procedural rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Local RunComfy token storage is disclosed
low_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores an API token under the user configuration directory with mode 0600. This is a credential-handling concern, but there is no evidence that the skill reads or exfiltrates the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path and permission mode are explicitly documented. The reviewed skill provides guidance only; no file-read or upload instruction targets the token.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are expected documentation and service endpoints
low_findings.description: The static network findings point to RunComfy pages, related skill pages, example CDN URLs, and documented service domains. No evidence found of unexpected telemetry or unrelated exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs align with the claimed RunComfy functionality and documentation. I did not find evidence of suspicious third-party collection endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak-crypto and reconnaissance detections are not confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and reconnaissance alerts appear to match ordinary prose, Markdown headings, model names, or documentation references. No evidence found of hashing code, system probing, or network scanning logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain descriptive routing or model guidance, not cryptographic or reconnaissance code. The file is a Markdown skill guide with no executable scanner implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash tool allow-list permits RunComfy command execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash access for runcomfy commands and relies on runcomfy run to execute external model workflows. This is expected for the skill, but it requires command and argument review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and workflow description both confirm command execution through runcomfy. The scope is limited to the named CLI.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote media URL inputs are passed to model APIs
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples pass image, audio, and video URLs into model requests. This can expose sensitive media to remote services and should be treated as network processing of user data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples show URL-based media inputs. The behavior is part of the intended workflow, not an inferred hidden behavior.

content.user_title: Swap Faces in RunComfy Media Workflows
content.value_statement: Face-swap projects need different models for still images, videos, batches, and motion transfer. This skill routes the request to a suitable RunComfy workflow and gives focused CLI guidance.
actual_capabilities: Selects a RunComfy route for still image, video, batch, motion-transfer, or prose-only face edits.
Provides RunComfy CLI command patterns for supported model endpoints.
Explains when to use Wan 2-2 Animate, Kling Motion Control, GPT Image 2 Edit, Nano Banana Edit, or Flux Kontext.
Documents relevant input fields such as image URLs, audio URLs, reference videos, prompts, aspect ratio, and resolution.
Includes consent, disclosure, privacy, token storage, and outbound endpoint guidance.
Lists troubleshooting exit codes and related RunComfy skills for adjacent workflows.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and valid access to the selected model endpoint.
Does not technically verify consent, identity rights, or platform disclosure requirements.
Uses remote URLs and external model APIs, so sensitive media may leave the local environment.
Does not run ComfyUI node-graph workflows directly through this skill.

use_cases.title: Create approved campaign stills
use_cases.description: Place an approved spokesperson face into a licensed scene while preserving pose, clothing, lighting, and background.

use_cases.title: Prototype consented video character swaps
use_cases.description: Choose a video route for a permitted character replacement, dubbed clip, or motion-transfer concept.

use_cases.title: Generate consistent identity variants
use_cases.description: Use a batch image route when one approved identity must appear across multiple product or narrative frames.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a route
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner selection for a simple task
prompt_templates.prompt: I have one approved portrait and one scene image. Choose the best RunComfy face-swap route and explain the required inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a still image swap
prompt_templates.scenario: Single output image with reference roles
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the face from my approved identity image in my target scene. Preserve pose, clothing, lighting, and background. Give the RunComfy command structure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a video character swap
prompt_templates.scenario: Video workflow with motion or audio constraints
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a licensed source performance video and an approved target character image. Pick the right video route and list the inputs I need.

prompt_templates.title: Design a batch identity workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I need the same approved identity across multiple campaign frames. Recommend the RunComfy route, input ordering, consistency settings, and review checks.

output_examples.input: A user asks to place an approved actor into one campaign hero image.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends GPT Image 2 Edit, assigns the scene as the base image and the portrait as the identity reference, and emphasizes preserving lighting and background.

output_examples.input: A user has a character image and wants it to follow motion from a licensed video clip.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Kling Motion Control Pro because the priority is preserving source motion rather than creating an audio-driven talking clip.

output_examples.input: A user wants the same approved face in several product gallery images.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Nano Banana Edit, places the identity image first, and suggests fixed aspect ratio and resolution for consistent results.

best_practices: Confirm consent, asset rights, and disclosure requirements before running any face-swap workflow.
Use clean, well-lit identity references and explicitly state which parts of the scene must stay unchanged.
Review outputs for identity errors, unwanted motion changes, and required synthetic-media labels before publication.
anti_patterns: Do not use the workflow for real people without documented permission.
Do not upload sensitive, private, defamatory, sexual, or illegal source material.
Do not run installation or authentication commands unless the user explicitly approves them.

faq.question: Does this skill create face swaps locally?
faq.answer: No. It routes requests through the RunComfy CLI and external RunComfy model APIs.

faq.question: Can it process both images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes separate routes for still images, batch image edits, video animation, and motion transfer.

faq.question: Does it verify consent automatically?
faq.answer: No. The operator must confirm consent, rights, and platform disclosure requirements before use.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: A RunComfy login or token is required for the CLI. The skill documents token storage and a CI environment variable option.

faq.question: Can it run any ComfyUI face-swap workflow?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on RunComfy model API routes and only links to broader ComfyUI workflow browsing.

faq.question: What should I do if a requested swap targets a public figure?
faq.answer: Refuse requests that lack consent or could create deceptive, defamatory, sexual, or harmful synthetic media.
